text:
Admission Date:  1980-06-22              Discharge Date:   2019-12-08

Date of Birth:  1988-04-02             Sex:   M

Service: CARDIOTHORACIC

Allergies:
Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs

Attending:Matthew
Chief Complaint:
Chest pain

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
1979-01-23 Aortic Valve Replacement(25mm [**Company 1543**] Mosaic Ultra
Porcine) and Five Vessel Coronary Artery Bypass Grafting(left
internal mammary artery to left anterior descending with vein
grafts to obtuse marginal, ramus, first diagonal and PDA)
1989-01-20 Re-exploration for Bleeding


History of Present Illness:
Mr. Lopez is a 67 year old male with known CAD, s/p anterior
MI in 1999 who presents with chest pain. Early this year patient
developed progressive decrease in his exercise tolerance, with
shortness of breath and chest tightness occuring with exertion
and resolving with rest. He was evaluated with a cardiac
catheterization that demonstrated 3vd and 90% occlusion of 1st
diagonal, reduced to 50% with POBA. Subsequently planned to
undergo CABG in 1999-10-19 by Dr. Cabrera. Following that
procedure his symptoms improved and chest pain had nearly
resolved. Then in late 1988-06-01 (~2 months ago) chest pain
returned and unlike previously it started occuring at rest, and
required longer rest periods to alleviate his discomfort. This
has continued a few times a day for the past 2 months. The day
of admission he was eating an afternoon meal and not exerting
himself patient had the acute onset of chest pain. The key
difference this time was that it was more intense, lasted
longer, and returned ~10 minutes after it subsided. Describes it
as his typical substernal chest tightness that radiated down
both arms. At the onset of symptoms patient took ASA 162mg,
lopressor and when pain did not resolve he took 2sl nitro.
Following the second SL nitro pain resolved but then returned
~10 minutes later. He knew he could only take 1 more nitro so he
presented to an OSH for evaluation as this was first time he had
required nitro for chest pain in some time. At OSH, ECG showed
poor R wave progression, but no other acute changes. CE's
negative. He was given lovenox and transferred to Kosair Children's Hospital for
further evaluation.

Past Medical History:
- Coronary artery disease, s/p MI 1999 - unsuccessful
angioplasty for mid LAD lesion in setting of anterior MI,
treated with streptokinase, POBA of diagonal in 06/1982
- Aortic Valve Stenosis
- GI Bleed after streptokinase in 1999
- Intermittent GERD
- Childhood Asthma
- Obesity
- s/p left tib/fib fracture
- Possible sleep apnea (has not had a study yet)
- LLE Venous Stasis Ulcer
- BLE varicosities
- BB gun injury posterior left leg
- Hemorrhoids, s/p hemorrhoidectomy
- s/p right fem. AV Fistula repair
- s/p Umbilical Hernia repair

Social History:
Patient is single without children. He lives with friend/partner
in South Texas Rehabilitation Hospital and Kelli Perez and is retired. Has a friend who
is involved with his care. He previously worked for the
telephone company. He denies ETOH and tobacco use.


Family History:
Father died from an MI at age 49. Brother died  from a cerebral
aneurysm at age 72.

Physical Exam:
VS: 97.5 149/82 57 20 99/3L (no home O2)
GENERAL: WDWN male in NAD. Oriented x3. Mood, affect
appropriate.
HEENT: NCAT. Sclera anicteric. PERRL, EOMI. Conjunctiva were
pink, no pallor or cyanosis of the oral mucosa. No xanthalesma.

NECK: Unable to appreciate elevated JVD
CARDIAC: PMI located in 5th intercostal space, midclavicular
line. RR, normal S1, S2. III/VI SEM at LUSB (AS), II/VI SM at
apex(?MR), No thrills, lifts. No S3 or S4. Murmurs radiate to
both R and L carotids.
LUNGS: No chest wall deformities, scoliosis or kyphosis. Resp
were unlabored, no accessory muscle use. CTAB, no crackles,
wheezes or rhonchi.
ABDOMEN: Soft, NTND. No HSM or tenderness. Abd aorta not
enlarged by palpation. No abdominial bruits.
EXTREMITIES: 2+ RLE edema, and 2+ LLE edema (but left leg
significantly greater girth, which pt says 12-04 previous fx in
1981 and bullet at some point). Has ulcer wrapped in gauze.
SKIN: No stasis dermatitis, ulcers, scars, or xanthomas.
PULSES:
Right: Carotid 2+ Femoral 2+ Popliteal 2+ DP 2+ PT 2+
Left: Carotid 2+ Femoral 2+ Popliteal 2+ DP 2+ PT 2+


Pertinent Results:
2019-12-08: INR 2.1 PTT 33.5 PT 22 Na 141 K 4.7 Cl 109 CO2 26 BUN
23 Crea 1.1 Glu 122 WBC 9.3 Hct 28.7 Plts 392
1980-06-22 WBC-4.7 RBC-4.93 Hgb-15.0 Hct-43.3 MCV-88 MCH-30.4
MCHC-34.6 RDW-14.5 Plt Ct-169
1980-06-22 PT-12.6 INR(PT)-1.1
1980-06-22 Glucose-99 UreaN-16 Creat-0.9 Na-144 K-4.7 Cl-106
HCO3-30 AnGap-13
1985-12-01 ALT-32 AST-32 AlkPhos-65 TotBili-0.6
1995-10-01 cTropnT-0.01
1980-06-22 CK-MB-6 cTropnT-0.02*
1970-03-16 CK-MB-5 cTropnT-<0.01
1970-03-16 Albumin-4.3 Calcium-9.1 Phos-3.8 Mg-2.1
1980-06-22 %HbA1c-6.3*
1980-06-22 Triglyc-112 HDL-39 CHOL/HD-3.2 LDLcalc-65
1980-06-22 Vein Mapping:
Greater and lesser saphenous veins were patent bilaterally.
1980-06-22 Carotid Ultrasound:
No significant stenosis identified (less than 40% bilaterally).
1985-12-01 Echocardiogram:
The left atrium is moderately dilated. There is mild symmetric
left ventricular hypertrophy with normal cavity size. There is
mild regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction with distal
anterolateral and anterior hypokinesis and apical dyskinesis.
There is an apical left ventricular aneurysm. Overall left
ventricular systolic function is normal (LVEF>55%). No masses or
thrombi are seen in the left ventricle. Right ventricular
chamber size and free wall motion are normal. The aortic root is
mildly dilated at the sinus level. The ascending aorta is mildly
dilated. The aortic arch is mildly dilated. The number of aortic
valve leaflets cannot be determined. The aortic valve leaflets
are moderately thickened. There is moderate aortic valve
stenosis (valve area 1.0-1.2cm2). Mild (1+) aortic regurgitation
is seen. The mitral valve leaflets are mildly thickened. There
is no mitral valve prolapse. The tricuspid valve leaflets are
mildly thickened. There is mild pulmonary artery systolic
hypertension. There is an anterior space which most likely
represents a fat pad.


Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. Lopez was initially admitted to the cardiology service
with unstable angina. He ruled out for myocardial infarction. He
remained pain free on intravenous Heparin and medical therapy.
In anticipation of surgical revascularization, Plavix was
discontinued. Prior to surgical intervention, extensive
preoperative evaluation was performed which included
echocardiogram, carotid ultrasound, vein mapping, and dental
clearance - please see result section for details. Preoperative
evaluation was notable for asymptomatic bacteruria which was
empirically treated with Ciprofloxacin(urine culture eventually
was negative). A monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined
significance was also diagnosed as SPEP showed abnormal band in
gamma region. There was no evidence of lytic lesions, no
leukocytosis, and no lymphadenopathy with normal calcium levels.
A follow-up SPEP in 11-28 months is recommended to monitor for
progression. In addition, he was followed closely by wound care
for his chronic lower extremity ulcer. His wound remained stable
and did show clinical improvement with adaptic dressing changes
daily. Preoperative course was otherwise unremarkable and he was
eventually cleared for surgery.

On 11-02, Dr. Cabrera performed an aortic valve
replacement and coronary artery bypass grafting surgery. Surgery
was complicated by coagulopathy which did not improve after
multiple blood products. On postoperative day zero, he returned
to the operating room for re-exploration. Please see operative
notes for surgical details. Once hemostasis was obtained, he
returned to the CVICU for invasive monitoring. Blood pressure
was initially labile and given evidence of [**Male First Name (un) **] on
echocardiogram, inotropes were avoided with goal to maintain
preload. Amiodarone was started for bouts of paroxysmal atrial
fibrillation, while pan-cultures were obtained for postop
fevers. Given fluid overload and hemodynamic instability, he was
kept intubated for several days.  Following aggressive diuresis,
he was eventually extubated on postoperative day three. By
postoperative day four, he required reintubation for respiratory
distress and increasing oxygen requirements. Bronchoscopy was
performed and cultures were obtained. Empiric antibiotics were
started for presumed pneumonia given persistent fevers,
infiltrate on chest x-ray, and right lower lobe secretions on
bronchoscopy. Given persistent bouts of atrial fibrillation,
Warfarin anticoagulation was also started.

On 2009-09-01 upon examination a sternal click was noted. A CT
scan was done which revealed sternal dehiscence. On 05-22 Mr.
Alexander Dyer was taken back to the operating room for sternal
debridement, rewiring and talon closure. See operative note for
details. Tissue cultures were obtained intraoperatively and
empiric vancomycin was started pending culture data. He was
again admitted to the ICU postoperatively for monitoring. He was
weaned and extubated without difficulty. He was transferred to
the stepdown unit for ongoing postoperative care. He remained in
atrial fibrillation and was anticoagulated with heparin drip
with bridge to Coumadin. His atrial fibrillation was treated
with amiodarone and Lopressor without conversion but was rate
controlled. Electrophysiology was consulted. It was decided to
hold off on cardioversion for now and continue anticoagulation
with Coumadin for atrial fibrillation. INR at the time of
discharge was 2.1 and he is to be given 5 mg Coumadin on 04-27
with further dosing to be determined for an INR goal of 2.0-3.0.
 Final gram stain of sternal tissue was negative the IV
vancomycin was stopped on 1988-10-10. His blood sugars were
covered by a insulin sliding scale and finger sticks ranged from
121-218.  The need to start an oral diabetic medication is to be
further evaluated by his PCP once his is out of the post
operative period. Mr. Lopez was evaluated and treated by
physical therapy for strength and conditioning. Rehab was
recommended upon discharge and he was transferred on post
operative day # 21 in stable condition.



Medications on Admission:
-Plavix 75 mg daily
-Imdur SR 30mg daily
-Lipitor 20 mg daily every evening
-Lopressor 100 mg three times a day
-Aspirin 162 mg twice a day
-MVI daily
-Vitamin E 400 IU daily
-Vitamin C 500 mg daily
-SL Nitroglycerin PRN


Discharge Medications:
1. Albuterol Sulfate 2.5 mg /3 mL (0.083 %) Solution for
Nebulization Sig: One (1)  Inhalation Q6H (every 6 hours) as
needed for wheezing.
2. Insulin Lispro 100 unit/mL Solution Sig: One (1)
Subcutaneous ASDIR (AS DIRECTED) FS 120-150 2 units humalog SC,
151-190 4 units humalog SC, 191-230 6 units humalog SC, 231-270
8 units humalog SC, > 270 Keith Smith MD
3. Atorvastatin 20 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY
(Daily).
4. Potassium Chloride 20 mEq Tab Sust.Rel. Particle/Crystal Sig:
One (1) Tab Sust.Rel. Particle/Crystal PO every twenty-four(24)
hours for 10 days.
5. Docusate Sodium 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO BID (2
times a day).
6. Amiodarone 200 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO BID (2 times
a day) for 2 weeks: then decrease to 200 mg po daily x 1 month
then reevaluate.
7. Acetaminophen 325 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO Q4H (every
4 hours) as needed for pain.
8. Magnesium Hydroxide 400 mg/5 mL Suspension Sig: Thirty (30)
ML PO HS (at bedtime) as needed for constipation.
9. Aspirin 81 mg Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) Sig: One (1)
Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) PO DAILY (Daily).
10. Bisacodyl 10 mg Suppository Sig: One (1) Suppository Rectal
DAILY (Daily) as needed for constipation.
11. Metoprolol Tartrate 25 mg Tablet Sig: Three (3) Tablet PO
QID (4 times a day).
12. Coumadin 2.5 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO once a day:
Dose for INR goal 2.0-3.0.
13. Lasix 40 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO once a day for 10
days.


Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care

Facility:
Hendrick Medical Center Northeast - Our Lady Of The Lake Regional Medical Center

Discharge Diagnosis:
PRIMARY: Aortic Valve Stenosis, Coronary Artery Disease
SECONDARY: Postop Acute Respiratory Failure, Postop Pneumonia,
Postop Atrial Fibrillation, Postop Bleeding, Preop Urinary Tract
Infection, Systolic Anterior Motion of Mitral Valve([**Male First Name (un) **]),
Monoclonal Gammopathy, Obesity, Sleep Apnea, GERD


Discharge Condition:
Alert and oriented x3 nonfocal
Ambulating, gait steady
Sternal pain managed with tylenol PRN


Discharge Instructions:
Please shower daily including washing incisions gently with mild
soap, no baths or swimming, and look at your incisions.
Please NO lotions, cream, powder, or ointments to incisions.
Each morning you should weigh yourself and then in the evening
take your temperature, these should be written down on the
chart.
No driving for approximately one month until follow up with
surgeon
No lifting more than 10 pounds for 10 weeks
Please call with any questions or concerns 20-892-9456.
While you were here we incidentally found a small abnormality in
your labs called a monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined
significance (MGUS). This is most likely completely benign, but
in a small number of people with MGUS (approximately 1% per
year) this can progress to a more serious condition. You should
also have your serum electrophoresis followed up in 11-28 months.
You should arrange to have this followed up with repeat labs in
11-28 months.

Followup Instructions:
Please call to schedule appointments:
Surgeon Dr. Cabrera (20-892-9456) 02-10 at 1:30 PM
Primary Care Dr. Reyes in 07-14 weeks
Cardiologist Dr. Pratt or Stewart in 07-14 weeks
Wound check appointment - Emergency Department 6 (918-94-4989) - your nurse
will schedule Remove staples in 3 weeks (01-14) and place steri
strips



Completed by:2019-12-08
